Investors have halved their position in Brent anticipating a pickup exports through the Strait of Hormuz as talks between the United States and Iran intensify and more vessels proceed through the waterway under cover of darkness.

Senior Israeli officials quoted in a Channel 12 report say that the emerging US-Iran deal indicates that Washington has agreed to Tehran’s “main conditions,” adding that “the Iranians are not agreeing to this for nothing.”

The report says Israeli officials and experts who have worked for decades on the Iran file consider that the terms of the MOU apparently set to be signed tomorrow “endanger Israel’s security interests.”

What will happen immediately “is the opening of the Strait of Hormuz, the resuscitation of the regime, and a slap in the face for the Iranian public,” the officials say.

Iran is “paying on credit” with this framework, the officials say, with Tehran only agreeing to discuss its nuclear program once the war formally ends and a number of other conditions are met, including the release of frozen Iranian funds.

“This is the same framework as the agreement to end the war in Gaza,” an official tells the TV station. “Ask yourself what has happened since then with the commitment to disarm Hamas? What will be the Americans’ essential leverage if, after 60 days of a ceasefire, the Iranians do not begin the steps required of them? The credible military threat has been all but eroded.”

Daily Market Report #764 TLDR

It is Saturday. Full World Cup fever here.

I am tipping most games on prediction markets and I am 2 out of 4 so far.

The $SPCX IPO happened yesterday, and it is all the market wants to talk about.

Trading around $160 today.

Pre-IPO perps had it at $180.

US equities added $1.15T in a day, Nasdaq up 3%.

Gold is still soft at $4,180, and the war premium leaving gold and oil tells you the market is de-risking the geopolitical trade.

$BTC is sitting at $63,487, basically flat.

Dominance 56.4%.

Spot BTC ETFs logged a fifth straight outflow day and have bled $2.1B since June started.

The institutional bid is still missing, but Morgan Stanley raised its Bitcoin holdings to 3,850 BTC via spot ETF.

$ETH is at $1,664, flat.

ETH TVL is up again, and 7-day flows are finally green across Ethereum, Solana, Arbitrum, and Base for the first time in over a month.

$SOL is at $66.73, flat, but it had the strongest fundamental week in my universe.

SPCX trading rails.

WSOP sponsorship.

Superteam Germany hosting the largest Solana event ever in Berlin.

Token doing nothing.

Ecosystem shipping hard.

$XRP is at $1.13.

JPMorgan and Ripple are testing 5-second XRP settlement, the kind of real-bank pilot XRP holders waited years for.

$HYPE is at $58.16.

Circle transferred $4B $USDC to Hyperliquid, an enormous liquidity vote even as the token cools.

$XMR is at $344, cooling after a huge week but still the privacy leader.

$ZEC is at $414.

AI is now both the auditor and the attacker, and after the Opus 4.8 audit found the Zcash bug, the security layer clearly has not caught up.

The collector itch is real.

Avalanche Treasury sank 38% on its Nasdaq debut, a reminder that the crypto-treasury-goes-public trade is not a guaranteed win.

NFTfi is shutting down as NFT lending contracts.

Japan passed a crypto bill classifying assets as financial instruments and cutting taxes, a real regulated-market unlock. $ADA is done. 

Bezos’s AI venture Prometheus raised $12B.

ChatGPT hit 1B monthly app users.

And developers are now saying Claude Fable 5 plays it too safe.

After I praised it earlier this week for fixing things I had not thought of, the broader dev consensus is that the safety guardrails went too far.

What are you watching this weekend?

quotedTweet.text:
Daily Market Report #764

It is Saturday. Full World Cup fever here.

I am tipping most of the games on prediction markets, and I am 2 out of 4 so far. Not a disaster, not a brag. Football is humbling in the same way trading is. You can read everything right and still watch a red card in the 12th minute torch your whole thesis. I will keep sharing the slips, wins and losses both.

The SpaceX IPO happened yesterday, and it is the only thing the market wants to talk about. Priced at $135, Elon is officially a trillionaire, and the stock is trading around $160 today after the pre-IPO perps had it at $180.

The detail I want to flag: Solana was the only platform where you could trade SPCX out of the gate yesterday. Not Hyperliquid, not a TradFi broker, Solana. On a day everyone was focused on Hyperliquid’s pre-IPO perps, Solana quietly had the rails working when it counted.

Stocks already ripped, US equities added $1.15T in a day, Nasdaq up 3%.

Gold $4,180, still soft. The war premium leaving gold and oil at the same time tells you this is real de-risking of the geopolitical trade, not fear.

Bitcoin $63,487, basically flat. Dominance 56.4%. Holding the range while everything around it moves. Spot BTC ETFs logged a fifth straight outflow day and have bled $2.1B since June started, with ETF volumes down 78% from peak.

The institutional bid is still missing.

But Morgan Stanley raised its Bitcoin holdings to 3,850 BTC via spot ETF, so it is not everyone leaving. It is rotation and thinning volume.

Saylor repeated his line again: never said Strategy would not sell.

He is going to keep saying it until people stop being angry, which will be never.

Metaplanet bought a Japanese securities firm to build BTC-linked products, the treasury-company-becomes-a-bank playbook continuing.

Ethereum $1,664, flat. ETH TVL up another 0.9%, and 7-day flows are finally green across Ethereum, Solana, Arbitrum, and Base for the first time in over a month.

The bleed has genuinely stopped at the chain level, even if price is flat.

Vitalik teased liquidation-free DeFi on the way, which would be a real structural shift if it ships.

Solana $66.73, flat, but the strongest story in my universe this week on fundamentals.

The SPCX trading rails, the WSOP sponsorship, and Superteam Germany hosting the largest Solana event ever in Berlin.

While the token does nothing, the Solana ecosystem is throwing the biggest parties and shipping the most product.

That gap usually resolves up eventually.

XRP $1.13, down 1%.

JPMorgan and Ripple are testing 5-second XRP settlement, which is the kind of real-bank pilot XRP holders have waited years for.

Sentiment at multi-month lows, usage down 91%, and the banks are quietly testing it anyway.

Hyperliquid $58.16, flat. Circle transferred $4B USDC to Hyperliquid, an enormous liquidity vote even as the token cools.

The Kalshi-perps competition story from yesterday still stands, but $4B in fresh stablecoin liquidity says the platform is not going anywhere.

Beaten token, thriving venue.

Monero $344, down 6.5%. Giving back some of the 18% week, but still the privacy leader.

Zcash $414, down 4.5%.

The privacy bid cools on a flat day, which is normal after a rip.

Immunefi’s CEO called AI the biggest new threat to DeFi smart contract security, which lands hard given the Opus 4.8 audit that found the Zcash bug.

AI is now both the auditor and the attacker, and the security layer has not caught up.

ARB unlock Tuesday and STRK unlock Monday, both worth watching into thin weekend liquidity.

Pengu $0.0067, and the Pudgy Vibes TCG Season 3 window is still open.

Avalanche Treasury sank 38% on its Nasdaq debut, a rough reminder that the crypto-treasury-goes-public trade is not a guaranteed win.

NFTfi is shutting down as the NFT lending market contracts, and that one stings as a collector.

The quiet infrastructure of the last NFT cycle is closing one door at a time.

Japan passed a crypto bill classifying assets as financial instruments and cutting taxes, a genuinely big regulated-market unlock.

SpaceX aside, the numbers stay surreal.

Bezos’s AI venture Prometheus raised $12B.

ChatGPT hit 1B monthly app users.

And the line that matters most: developers are now saying Claude Fable 5 plays it too safe.

After I praised it earlier this week for fixing things I had not thought of, the broader dev consensus is that the safety guardrails went too far.

Anthropic walked back the sabotage policy, and the market is telling them the model is too cautious. It’s crazy also that US stoped Fable.

Fascinating problem to have, building something so capable that the debate is whether it is too careful.

What are you watching this weekend?

Mid Afternoon Dockside Dispatch – Saturday, June 13, 2026

Cainhoy Peninsula, South Carolina. From the Cainhoy dock.

Mid-afternoon light sits steady over the Wando as the tide runs full and pluff mud warms at the edges. A great blue heron stands its ground in the shallows while Spartina ripples in the breeze. The fixed perch holds.

Quick Pulse

SPX near 7580
VIX near 19
Brent Crude near 93
DXY near 99.3
Geopol Whisper

Public signaling moved forward today. President Trump posted that the deal is scheduled to be signed tomorrow and that the Strait of Hormuz will be open to all immediately afterward. U.S. officials have expressed 80 to 85 percent confidence that an agreement will be reached in the coming days, with a signed memorandum of understanding expected to open a 60-day period of technical negotiations.

Iranian messaging remains mixed. The Foreign Minister previously stated a settlement has never been closer, while other officials continue to describe the situation as not yet finalized. No confirmed text has been released and no explicit Supreme Leader approval has been publicly reported. Conflicting accounts of the draft’s terms persist, particularly around nuclear commitments, Hormuz management, and the sequencing of any sanctions relief.

The pause holds for now, but the distance between optimistic timeline talk and a mutually understood, signed framework has not closed.

Lowcountry Lesson

The Wando does not change its course because someone upstream declares the tide has turned. It keeps its own rhythm, revealing over time what actually settles against the pilings and what only claims to move. Position belongs to the one who stays fixed long enough to separate passing declarations from what truly lands.

Grateful for every watcher holding position.

DYOR. NFA.
Throne steady. Kingdom vigilant. Unbroken. 🔥
King of Cainhoy 🇺🇸🔥
Operation Lowcountry Wildfire Intelligence
Cainhoy Peninsula, South Carolina

World Bank Cuts Global Growth Forecast to 2.5% for 2026, the Weakest Since the Pandemic

The World Bank expects global growth to slow to 2.5% in 2026, the weakest pace since the onset of the pandemic, as the conflict in the Middle East lifts energy prices, inflation, and borrowing costs.

📌 Global growth 2.5% in 2026 (from 2.9% in 2025), recovering to 2.8% in 2027, with forecasts cut for two-thirds of economies
📌 Brent crude assumed near $94 a barrel in 2026, about 36% above 2025, lifting global inflation to 4.0%
📌 In a severe scenario, global growth could fall to just 1.3% and inflation rise to 4.4%
📌 Gulf economies directly affected slow from 3.9% in 2025 to close to zero in 2026, then rebound to about 5% in 2027-28
📌 The wider Middle East and North Africa region grows 1.6% in 2026 before recovering to 5.0% in 2027
📌 The World Bank Group stands ready to provide up to $100 billion for affected countries over 15 months

For the Gulf, the report frames the downturn as sharp but temporary, with a strong rebound once trade normalises and reconstruction begins. Its emphasis on sovereign wealth funds, fiscal rules, and diversification mirrors the tools the GCC has been building.

Gemini: 

It is entirely accurate that President Trump has repeatedly claimed a resolution to the ongoing conflict with Iran is imminent, and the surrounding circumstances have sparked intense scrutiny regarding market manipulation and insider trading.
Since the U.S. and Israeli strikes began in late February 2026, media outlets and analysts have tracked Trump publicly predicting an imminent deal, ceasefire, or settlement **at least 38 to 39 times**. Because the conflict severely disrupted global energy supplies by virtually shutting down the Strait of Hormuz, any news regarding peace drastically impacts the global economy.
Here is a breakdown of why these announcements have led to widespread allegations of stock market manipulation and corruption.
### The Immediate Market Impact
Trump’s declarations—often made abruptly on his Truth Social platform—reliably trigger massive, immediate swings in the financial sector:
 * **Oil Prices:** The prospect of the Strait of Hormuz reopening causes the price of crude oil to plummet.
 * **Equities:** Relief over lowering energy costs and easing inflation fears routinely sends the stock market surging, including a recent instance where the Dow Jones Industrial Average jumped over 1,000 points following a post.
### Suspicious Trading Activity
The core of the manipulation allegations stems from highly irregular, perfectly timed trades executed just moments before Trump's market-moving posts.
 * **The "Monday Morning" Trades:** Financial experts noted that just minutes—and in some cases, seconds—before a sudden Monday morning announcement by Trump claiming a "great settlement" had been reached, massive bets were placed.
 * **Hundreds of Millions on the Line:** According to market data analysis, roughly 6,200 Brent and West Texas Intermediate (WTI) futures contracts changed hands, representing a notional value of **$580 million**. Simultaneously, massive purchases of S&P 500 futures were executed.
 * **Prediction Market Anomalies:** There have also been highly profitable, suspiciously timed bets placed on prediction platforms like Polymarket by newly created, anonymous accounts regarding U.S. military actions and ceasefires just before they were announced.
### Reactions and Denials
Because there were no scheduled government economic releases or public catalysts to trigger these massive trades prior to the posts, critics argue the only logical explanation is that someone with advance knowledge of Trump's social media activity used that inside information to generate instant profits.
 * **Iranian Denials:** Iranian officials have repeatedly pushed back on Trump's claims. Iran's parliament speaker, Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, publicly denied that any productive negotiations were taking place, explicitly accusing the U.S. president of peddling "fake news" specifically to "manipulate the financial and oil markets."
 * **Accusations of Corruption:** Economists like Paul Krugman and lawmakers such as Senator Chris Murphy have publicly questioned the timing of these trades, asking if family members, White House staffers, or close associates were involved, with Murphy calling the situation "mind-blowing corruption."
Despite the 39 separate claims of an impending resolution, a finalized and signed peace agreement has yet to materialize, leaving the markets highly volatile and susceptible to sudden shifts based on the president's statements.

The SPR (Strategic Petroleum Reserve) has bought time, not barrels for refiners. US commercial crude inventories drew more than 10 million barrels in the latest EIA week — one of the sharpest weekly draws on record — on top of six prior consecutive weeks of above-estimate declines. Diplomatic resolution doesn't flip that overnight; tanker re-routing back through Hormuz adds weeks of physical lag. The physical market is tighter now than it was before the SPR started releasing.

🇮🇷🇺🇸 🕊 US-Iran Deal Nears Finalization
A US-Iran memorandum of understanding is expected to be signed electronically within 24 hours, mediated by Pakistan and Qatar. The deal would extend the ceasefire by 60 days, reopen the Strait of Hormuz, and initiate nuclear talks.

🇮🇷🇺🇸 🚨 Iran Secures Uranium Stockpile
Iran has begun collapsing underground tunnels and rigging entrances with explosives to secure its near weapons-grade uranium stockpile. This action makes the material significantly more difficult and dangerous to access, complicating any future agreement requiring its surrender.

🇮🇷🇸🇦 💥 Iranian Strikes on US Bases in Bahrain and Kuwait
Satellite imagery confirms the destruction of two Fuel System Supply Points (FSSP) at Isa Air Base in Bahrain and an Early Warning Radar system at Ali Al Salem Air Base in Kuwait following Iranian missile/drone strikes on June 11.

🇺🇦🇷🇺 💥 Ukrainian Strikes on Crimean Logistics
Ukrainian drones struck the Crimean Titan chemical plant in Armyansk and damaged the Chongar road bridge and Syvash railway bridge, disrupting Russian logistics in the Kherson region. Pontoon bridges are also being targeted.

🇮🇱🇱🇧 ⚔️ IDF Operations in Southern Lebanon
The IDF struck over 70 Hezbollah targets in southern Lebanon, destroying launchers and structures. Seven Hezbollah operatives were killed, and maps illustrating threats to northern Israeli communities were found in a tunnel network near Beaufort Ridge.

🇮🇷🇺🇸 ⚓ Strait of Hormuz Blockade and Tanker Strike
CENTCOM forces have redirected 141 commercial vessels and disabled 9 since the blockade on Iranian ports began. An oil tanker, reportedly US-escorted, was struck by an unknown projectile near Oman after disabling its AIS and ignoring IRGC-approved routes.

🇮🇳 ✈️ Indian Air Force Transport Crash
An Indian Air Force transport aircraft crashed during a landing attempt at an air base, killing five crew members.

🇮🇷💻 Cyberattack on Iranian Banks
Four major Iranian banks (Melli, Tejarat, Saderat, and Export Development Bank) experienced service disruptions, affecting mobile banking, online banking, ATMs, and card payments. Authorities state no data was compromised and systems are being restored.

quotedTweet.text:
Hi @anasalhajji, NOONE with a real BRAIN as of June 2026 can still ASSUME that The New Hitler Trump admin is opposed to Ukraine striking Russian refineries and ports out of concern for...... whatever!....Those of us who are watching this war DAY AND NIGHT  carefully know very well that the Ukrainian attacks on Russian refineries during Trump admin surged significantly in 2005  and finally  Adam Entous of the NYT (an investigative journalist whom I trust a lot) exposed the scheme behind these attacks in his 30 December 2025 piece in the NYT.  HERE IS WHAT ENTOUS WROTE:

I QUOTE: 

...Where Mr. Hegseth had marginalized his Ukraine-supporting generals, the C.I.A. director, Mr. Ratcliffe, had consistently protected his own officers’ efforts for Ukraine. He kept the agency’s presence in the country at full strength; funding for its programs there even increased. When Mr. Trump ordered the March aid freeze, the U.S. military rushed to shut down all intelligence sharing. But when Mr. Ratcliffe explained the risk facing C.I.A. officers in Ukraine, the White House allowed the agency to keep sharing intelligence about Russian threats inside Ukraine.

Now, the agency honed a plan to at least buy time, to make it harder for the Russians to capitalize on the Ukrainians’ extraordinary moment of weakness.

One powerful tool finally employed by the Biden administration — supplying ATACMS and targeting intelligence for strikes inside Russia — had been effectively pulled from the table. But a parallel weapon had remained in place — permission for C.I.A. and military officers to share targeting intelligence and provide other assistance for Ukrainian drone strikes against crucial components of the Russian defense industrial base. These included factories manufacturing “energetics” — chemicals used in explosives — as well as petroleum-industry facilities.

Suited men sitting at a table with the American flag in the background.
John Ratcliffe, the C.I.A. chief, looked on during a White House meeting in July. Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
In the Trump administration’s first months, these strikes had been scattershot with negligible impact. Ukrainian military and intelligence agencies were competing, working off different target lists. Russia’s air defenses and electromagnetic jammers rendered energetics facilities virtually impenetrable. At oil refineries, drones were slamming into storage tanks, igniting blasts that grabbed headlines but accomplished little else.

In June, beleaguered U.S. military officers met with their C.I.A. counterparts to help craft a more concerted Ukrainian campaign. It would focus exclusively on oil refineries and, instead of supply tanks, would target the refineries’ Achilles’ heel: A C.I.A. expert had identified a type of coupler that was so hard to replace or repair that a refinery would remain offline for weeks. (To avoid backlash, they would not supply weapons and other equipment that Mr. Vance’s allies wanted for other priorities.)

As the campaign began to show results, Mr. Ratcliffe discussed it with Mr. Trump. The president seemed to listen to him; they had a frequent Sunday tee time. According to U.S. officials, Mr. Trump praised America’s surreptitious role in these blows to Russia’s energy industry. They gave him deniability and leverage, he told Mr. Ratcliffe, as the Russian president continued to “jerk him off.”

The energy strikes would come to cost the Russian economy as much as $75 million a day, according to one U.S. intelligence estimate. The C.I.A. would also be authorized to assist with Ukrainian drone strikes on “shadow fleet” vessels in the Black Sea and the Mediterranean. Gas lines would start forming across Russia.

“We found something that is working,” a senior U.S. official said, then had to add, “How long, we don’t know.”

END OF QUOTE.

51 years ago, in March of 1975, an unnamed “Washington-based professor and defense consultant with intimate links to high-level U.S. policymakers” penned an article in Harper’s titled “Seizing Arab Oil.” Under the pseudonym “Miles Ignotus,” Latin for unknown soldier, the author calls for 9 infantry battalions from the 82nd and an entire Marine Division to invade Saudi Arabia and occupy their oil fields in order to break OPEC. 

The U.S. would then orchestrate the sale of Saudi oil fields on the world market. In the author’s words: “an occupation of 10 years and probably much less would suffice,” wherein the “remaining OPEC members would see reason and accept a binding commitment to maintain supplies at agreed prices in exchange for American withdrawal.”

That unnamed author was Edward Luttwak.
